🚴 You're cycling at 10 m/s down a neighborhood street when a child suddenly runs out chasing a ball. You slam the brakes. Newton's 2nd law says F = ma — the brake pads squeeze the rim, applying a force that decelerates you.

Your speed drops from 10 m/s to 0 in just 2 seconds. That rate of change is acceleration: a = (0 − 10)/2 = −5 m/s². Your body lurches forward — that's inertia fighting the change. The negative sign? You're slowing down, which means the force is backward.

You + your bike = 80 kg. F = 80 × (0 − 10)/2 = −400 N. Distribute the mass: F = (80×0 − 80×10)/2 = (0 − 800)/2. Your brakes are applying 400 N of force — about the weight of a small adult pushing against your motion!

Momentum p = mv captures your "quantity of motion." You started with p = 80 × 10 = 800 kg·m/s. Over 2 seconds, that momentum drops to zero. So F = Δp/Δt = (0 − 800)/2 = −400 N. Force is simply how fast momentum changes. This is why airbags save lives — they increase impact time Δt, which reduces the force F!
Airbags extend the time of impact during a crash — increasing Δt from 0.01s to 0.1s reduces the force by 10×, which is why airbags save lives by reducing peak force on occupants.

Derivation: Recoil Velocity of a Gun
Follow how conservation of momentum gives the recoil formula
The gun recoils opposite to the bullet's direction

🎯 You're at a shooting range, a 4 kg rifle resting on your shoulder. You breathe, aim at the target, and squeeze the trigger. The gun + bullet form an "isolated system" — all the momentum before firing must equal all the momentum after.

Before firing, the rifle and the 10 g bullet are both completely still in your hands. Total initial momentum = 0. Everything is quiet, steady — zero motion, zero momentum. Then the firing pin strikes.
BANG! The bullet rockets forward at 400 m/s. Its momentum: 0.01 × 400 = 4 kg·m/s forward. By conservation, the gun must have −4 kg·m/s momentum backward into your shoulder: m_b·v_b + m_g·v_g = 0.

Solving: v_g = −(m_b·v_b)/m_g = −(0.01 × 400)/4 = −1 m/s. The negative sign? The gun kicks backward into your shoulder at 1 m/s. A heavier gun recoils less — that's why competition shooters use massive rifles. Rockets work the same way: exhaust shoots down, the rocket goes up!
Firefighters must brace their footing before using a hose — the water shooting forward creates equal backward force (recoil), just like a rocket expelling gas to move in space.

Derivation: Orbital Speed of a Satellite
Derive v = 2πr/T from basic definitions of speed and circular motion

🛰️ Imagine throwing a baseball horizontally from the top of a 100 km tall mountain. It arcs downward and lands far away. The further you want it to go, the faster you must throw. Average speed = distance / time — this simple idea is the seed of orbital mechanics.

Now imagine throwing at 7.8 km/s — so fast that the Earth curves away beneath the ball at the same rate it falls! The ball never hits the ground — it orbits. In one full orbit, it travels the circumference of a circle: d = 2πr, where r is the distance from Earth's center.
At 408 km above Earth (the ISS altitude), one complete orbit takes T = 92.7 minutes — that's the orbital period. The ISS crew sees 16 sunrises and sunsets every single day because they orbit so fast.

v = 2πr/T = 2π × 6,779,000 m / 5,562 s ≈ 7,660 m/s — over 27,000 km/h! Higher orbits are slower: the Moon at 384,000 km orbits at just 1 km/s. The higher you go, the more leisurely your journey around Earth.
Geostationary satellites at 35,786 km orbit once every 24 hours, matching Earth's rotation so satellite TV dishes never need to move. Just 3 satellites can cover the entire globe.
ISS orbits at 408 km altitude. Earth radius = 6,371 km. T = 92.7 minutes.
Correct! ~7,658 m/s. The ISS travels at 7.66 km/s — over 27,000 km/h, orbiting Earth 16 times per day!

How P = ρgh emerges from the definition of pressure and weight

🏊 You jump off a boat into the ocean and dive down. Your ears feel an uncomfortable pressure building. Pressure = force / area — the column of water above you has weight, and that weight pushes on every square millimeter of your body.

The force is the weight of the water: F = mg. But m = ρV (density × volume). Seawater density is 1,025 kg/m³ — it's heavier than fresh water, which is why floating in the ocean is easier! F = ρ × V × g.

The volume of water above you = area of your body × depth: V = A × h. So F = ρ × A × h × g. Notice the area A appears — a wider person has more water weight above them. But something magical happens next...

P = F/A = (ρ × A × h × g)/A = ρgh. The area CANCELS! Pressure at a given depth is the same for everyone regardless of body shape. At just 10 m deep, pressure doubles to 2 atmospheres. Free divers train for years to handle this — the pressure crushes air spaces like your lungs and sinuses.
V = A × h, so P = (ρ × A × h × g) / A
Submarines are built with thick hulls because at 300 m depth, water pressure is 30 atmospheres — enough to crush ordinary steel. The deeper you go, the more pressure increases.

How Pascal's principle lets a small force lift a heavy load
Output force = area ratio × input force

🔧 You're a mechanic with a 1500 kg car that needs lifting. You place a hydraulic jack under the frame and pump the handle. Blaise Pascal discovered that pressure applied to a fluid in a closed container is transmitted equally in all directions. The pressure you create at the handle reaches the car undiminished.
Pushing the small piston (area = 0.005 m²) with just 240 N of force creates P₁ = 240/0.005 = 48,000 Pa — like pressing a pen tip into your palm. A small area means big pressure from a small force.

By Pascal's principle, the 48,000 Pa you created at the pump handle is transmitted through the hydraulic fluid WITHOUT LOSING ANY STRENGTH. P₂ = P₁ = 48,000 Pa. The fluid carries your force invisibly to where it's needed.

F₂/A₂ = F₁/A₁, so F₂ = (A₂/A₁) × F₁ = (0.25/0.005) × 240 = 12,000 N! Your puny 240 N push lifts a full car. Mechanical advantage = 50×. This same principle stops your car when you press the brake pedal, controls massive excavators, and even powers dentist chairs!
Car brakes use hydraulic multiplication — a small force on the brake pedal (5 cm² piston) creates 20× more force on the brake pads (100 cm² piston), letting your foot stop a 2-ton car.
Hydraulic lift: A₁ = 0.005 m², A₂ = 0.25 m². Car weight = 12,000 N.
What minimum input force is needed?
Correct! F₁ = 240 N. With a 50:1 mechanical advantage, 240 N lifts 12,000 N — that's the power of hydraulics!

How Eₖ = ½mv² comes from work and Newton's second law
Kinetic energy = half × mass × velocity squared

🛹 You're at the top of a massive half-pipe, heart pounding. You drop in, and gravity takes over. The work gravity does on you transforms into your kinetic energy: E_k = Work = F × d. The steeper the drop, the more work gravity does.

Gravity pulls you with force F = mg. The distance d you travel relates to your speed. Starting from rest, your average speed down the ramp is v_avg = v/2. So d = v_avg × t = (v/2) × t. E_k = (ma)(d) = m × a × (v/2 × t).

Your acceleration down the ramp is a = v/t (how quickly you reach speed v in time t). Plug it in: E_k = m × (v/t) × (v/2 × t). The t appears in the numerator AND denominator. Something beautiful is about to happen...

The time cancels! E_k = m × v × v/2 = ½mv². Doubling your speed means QUADRUPLING your energy. This is why car crashes at 100 km/h are 4× as destructive as at 50 km/h, and why dropping from twice the height on a skateboard doesn't just hurt twice as much — it hurts four times as much!

A car at 100 km/h has 4× the kinetic energy of the same car at 50 km/h. This is why speed limits exist — stopping distance grows with the square of speed, and crash energy quadruples when speed doubles.
A 1000 kg car travels at 20 m/s (72 km/h).

How Eₚ = mgh comes from the definition of work

🎢 You're strapped into a roller coaster car, clicking up the first massive hill. The chain lift mechanism groans as it pulls you higher. Every meter you rise stores energy. E_p = Work = F × S — the motor does work against gravity to lift you.

The force needed to lift the 500 kg car (plus you!) at constant speed equals its weight: F = mg. With two passengers, total mass ≈ 700 kg, so F = 700 × 9.81 = 6,867 N — about the weight of a small car.

The first hill towers 45 meters above the ground — that's the vertical height S = h. The motor's work: W = 6,867 × 45 = 309,015 J. Every joule is stored as gravitational potential energy, waiting to be released.

E_p = mgh = 700 × 9.81 × 45 = 309,015 J — enough energy to light a 60W bulb for 85 minutes! At the crest, you pause for a split second... then you plunge. That stored PE converts into kinetic energy as you race down, powering you through loops and corkscrews. The higher the first hill, the more energy for the entire ride!
Hydroelectric dams store water at height — every kilogram of water at 100 m has 981 J of gravitational PE, converted to electricity as it falls through turbines.

Newton's First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia): An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force. Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. The more mass an object has, the more inertia it has.

Static friction is not broken; the coin moves with the card.

Deriving the ultrasound depth formula from basic speed
Depth = (Speed × Time) / 2 — used in sonar & ultrasound

You're a sonar operator on a research ship mapping the ocean floor. You send a powerful ping into the deep. Speed = distance over time — the sound pulse travels through water at 1500 m/s, searching for the bottom.
The ping hits the ocean floor and reflects back up. The total journey is there AND back: D = 2d. Your sound travels twice the target distance. You wait, listening for the echo...
Your equipment measures exactly 0.4 seconds between sending the ping and receiving the echo. v = 2d/t means 1500 = 2d/0.4. The sound traveled 600 m total.
d = v x t / 2 = 1500 x 0.4 / 2 = 300 m. Divide by 2 because the sound went down AND back up! This same formula maps the ocean floor, detects submarines, and creates ultrasound images.
Bats use echolocation — they emit ultrasonic chirps and listen for echoes. A 17 kHz squeak bouncing off a moth 1 m away returns in 6 ms, letting the bat pinpoint prey in total darkness.
A ship sends a sonar pulse. The echo returns after 0.4 s. Sound in water = 1500 m/s.
Find the depth of the ocean floor.
Correct! 300 m. The total distance traveled is 600 m (there and back), so the ocean floor is 300 m deep.
Not quite. Remember to divide by 2! d = v×t/2 = 1500×0.4/2
Deriving θc from Snell's Law for Total Internal Reflection
Critical angle determines the threshold for total internal reflection

Swimming underwater, you look up at the surface. The world above appears as a shimmering circle. Light bends as it leaves water (n=1.33) into air (n=1.0). Snell's law n₁ sinθᵢ = n₂ sinθr describes exactly how much it bends.

As you swim deeper and look up at a steeper angle, the bright circle shrinks. At a certain angle, the refracted ray runs along the surface at 90 degrees. This is the critical angle — beyond it, you can no longer see above the water.
With sin 90 = 1, Snell's law simplifies: n₁ sinθc = n₂. The water surface turns into a perfect mirror. Below the critical angle, total internal reflection takes over.

Diamonds sparkle because they have a high refractive index (n=2.42), giving a critical angle of only 24°. Light entering gets trapped by total internal reflection, bouncing inside before escaping — creating brilliant fire!
Glass has n = 1.5 and air is n = 1.0. Find the critical angle for the glass-air boundary.
Correct! 41.8°. Beyond this angle, light totally reflects inside glass — the principle behind optical fibers!
Adjust refractive indices to see critical angle change.
Derivation: Universal Wave Equation v = fλ
Deriving the universal wave equation from basic speed formula
Speed = Frequency × Wavelength — universal for all waves
You're at the beach watching waves roll in. Wave speed = distance over time: v = d/t.
In one complete wave cycle, a wave travels exactly one wavelength in one time period T. Substituting: v = λ/T.
Frequency f = 1/T. Higher frequency means more waves per second. Light waves have enormous frequencies — 5e14 Hz for green light.
v = f × λ. This is the UNIVERSAL wave equation — it works for ALL waves. Sound, light, radio, water waves all obey v = fλ!
Tuning a guitar string to 82 Hz (low E) with length 0.65 m means the wave speed is 107 m/s. Tightening the string raises the wave speed, increasing frequency — that's how you tune a guitar.
A sound wave has f = 440 Hz (middle A) and λ = 0.78 m. Find the speed of sound.
Correct! ~343 m/s. That's the speed of sound in air at 20°C!
Deriving power formula from voltage and current definitions
Power = Voltage × Current — the rate of energy transfer in a circuit
Power tells you how fast electrical energy is being used. A 60W bulb converts 60 J of electrical energy into light and heat every second. Power = Energy / Time — the rate of energy transfer.
Voltage V is the energy per unit charge: V = E/Q. One volt means 1 J of energy per coulomb of charge. A 12V battery gives 12 J of energy to each coulomb that flows.
Current I is the charge flowing per second: I = Q/t. One ampere = 1 coulomb per second. The total charge that flows carries the energy that gets converted.
P = E/t = (V × Q)/t = V × (Q/t) = V × I. Power = Voltage × Current. A 60W bulb at 120V draws I = P/V = 0.5 A. Your phone charger at 5V, 2A delivers 10W of power.
Power companies charge for kilowatt-hours (kWh). 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ. A 100W bulb left on for 10 hours uses 1 kWh. Your monthly bill = total kWh × rate.
A 1.5 kW kettle runs on 240 V. How much current does it draw?
Correct! I = 6.25 A. That's why kettles need thick wires — they draw high current!
Deriving energy from power and time
Energy = Power × Time — how much electricity you consume
Power tells us how fast energy is used. A 2000W air conditioner consumes energy at 2000 J per second. The longer it runs, the more energy it consumes.
P = E/t, so E = P × t. If you run the AC for 1 hour (3600 s), E = 2000 × 3600 = 7,200,000 J. That's a lot of energy!
Since P = V × I, we can write E = V × I × t. A 2000W AC running on 240V draws I = P/V = 2000/240 = 8.33 A. E = VIt = 240 × 8.33 × 3600 = 7.2 MJ.
E = VIt. This combines voltage, current, and time into one formula for electrical energy. 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh) = 3.6 MJ. Your electricity bill is based on kWh consumed.
A typical UK home uses about 10 kWh per day. That's 36 MJ of electrical energy! Solar panels on your roof generate about 1 kWh per hour of peak sunlight per square meter.
A 100W LED bulb runs for 5 hours. How much energy does it consume in kWh?
Correct! 0.5 kWh. That's about 1.8 MJ of energy.
Not quite. E = P × t = 100 × 5 = 500 Wh. Convert to kWh by dividing by 1000.
Derivation: Real vs Apparent Expansion of Liquids

On a hot summer day, you check the outdoor thermometer. The red liquid climbs up the glass tube. But here is the trick: the glass itself also expands. You are only seeing the APPARENT expansion of the liquid.

Gamma-r = Gamma-a + Gamma-g. The real expansion of the liquid (Gamma-r) equals what you observe (Gamma-a) plus the glass flask expansion (Gamma-g). The glass hides some of the liquid's true expansion.
Since Gamma-r is greater than Gamma-a always, the real expansion is always bigger than what you see. The glass container grows too, making extra room for the liquid.
Thermometers use mercury or alcohol expansion — a 1°C rise expands liquid up a narrow capillary. Mercury thermometers work from -39°C to 357°C.
A liquid's apparent expansion is γ_a = 1.2×10⁻⁴/°C. Glass expansion is γ_g = 2.5×10⁻⁵/°C. Find γ_r.
Correct! 1.45×10⁻⁴/°C. Real expansion is always larger than apparent.
Adjust expansion coefficients to see real vs apparent relationship.
Deriving α the coefficient of linear thermal expansion
On a scorching summer day, train tracks can buckle under the heat. The change in length is proportional to original length and temperature change.
Engineers use expansion joints to prevent buckling. α is the coefficient of linear expansion. Steel has α = 1.2e-5 per °C.
α = ΔL/(L₀·ΔT). A 100 m rail heating from 15°C to 45°C expands by 3.6 cm.
L = L₀(1 + α·ΔT). Invar barely expands — used in precision instruments. Bimetallic strips in thermostats use two metals to bend with heat.
Railway tracks have expansion gaps — steel expands by 0.012 mm/m/°C. A 1 km track on a 40°C day grows 12 cm longer than in winter.
A steel rail is 20 m long at 15°C. In summer it reaches 45°C. α_steel = 1.2×10⁻⁵/°C. How much does it expand?
Correct! 7.2 mm. Over 1 km of rail, that's 36 cm — why expansion gaps exist!
Not quite. ΔL = α × L₀ × ΔT. ΔT = 45-15 = 30°C.
Adjust values to see how rods expand with temperature.
Deriving M = I/O = q/p from geometry
Magnification = Image height / Object height = Image distance / Object distance
You're examining a tiny antique watch with a magnifying glass. Magnification M = I/O. The lens makes a small thing look big.
From ray diagram geometry, similar triangles reveal M = q/p. The lens creates a virtual image that your brain interprets as larger.
M = I/O = q/p. For a concave mirror, q is negative for real images.
Microscopes use two lenses — objective creates magnified real image, eyepiece magnifies further. Compound microscopes achieve 1000× magnification.
An object 2 cm tall is placed 15 cm from a concave mirror. Image forms 30 cm in front. Find magnification and image height.
Correct! I = 4 cm (inverted). M = -2 means twice as tall and inverted!
M = q/p = 30/15 = 2. Image height = M×O = 2×2 = 4 cm

You flip a switch and the ceiling light turns on. Add another lamp — both stay bright because they are wired in parallel. Total current splits among branches: I = I₁ + I₂ + I₃.
Using Ohm's law for each branch: V/Req = V/R₁ + V/R₂ + V/R₃. Voltage is the same across all parallel components.
Divide by V: 1/Req = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃. For two 100Ω resistors: 1/Req = 2/100, so Req = 50Ω. Adding paths DECREASES resistance.
House wiring is parallel so each outlet gets full 230V independently. Unplugging one doesn't cut power to others.
Two resistors 100Ω and 100Ω are in parallel. Find equivalent resistance.
Correct! 50Ω. Two equal resistors in parallel give half the value.
Adjust resistors to see parallel equivalent.
Deriving E = hf = hc/λ and p = h/λ
Photon energy and momentum from Planck and de Broglie
Max Planck discovered light comes in tiny packets called photons. E = hf — each photon's energy equals Planck constant times frequency.
For light, c = fλ so f = c/λ. Red light (700nm) has low frequency. UV light (below 380nm) has higher frequency — more energetic photons.
Remote controls use infrared LEDs at 940 nm — each photon carries 2.1×10⁻¹⁹ J. Your TV detects thousands per second.
Green light has f = 5.45×10¹⁴ Hz. h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴. Find photon energy.
Correct! 3.61×10⁻¹⁹ J (2.25 eV). Photon energy is incredibly tiny at human scale!
Adjust frequency to see photon energy and momentum.
Output voltage depends on the ratio of resistances
You turn the volume knob on your speakers. Inside is a potentiometer — a variable resistor that divides voltage. I = V_in/(R₁+R₂).
The voltage you want is across R₂. Using Ohm's law: V_out = I × R₂. Same current flows through both resistors.
V_out = R₂/(R₁+R₂) × V_in. The voltage divides proportionally to the resistance ratio. Turn the knob — volume changes!
Volume knobs in audio equipment are potential dividers — sliding contact moves along a resistive track, controlling loudness.
A 9V battery with R₁ = 1kΩ, R₂ = 500Ω. What is V_out across R₂?
Adjust resistors to see how voltage divides.
Deriving exponential decay law N = N₀/2ⁿ
Exponential decay law for radioactive nuclei
An archaeologist discovers an ancient wooden tool. Carbon-14 dating holds the answer. After one half-life (5,730 years), exactly half remain: N₁ = ½ N₀.
After two half-lives (11,460 years), only a quarter remains: N₂ = (½)² N₀.
After n half-lives: N = (½)ⁿ N₀ = N₀/2ⁿ. This is exponential decay.
N = N₀/2^(t/T½). If the tool has 25% of original C-14, n = 2 half-lives = 11,460 years old.
Smoke detectors contain americium-241 (432-year half-life). It ionizes air between plates; smoke disrupts this, triggering the alarm.
A sample starts with 800 nuclei, half-life = 5 years. How many remain after 15 years?
Adjust half-life and time to see exponential decay.
Deriving resistivity from resistance and geometry
An electrician wires a new house. R ∝ L/A. Longer wires resist more; thicker wires resist less.
R = ρ × L/A. ρ is resistivity — the material's inherent opposition to current. Copper ρ = 1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m.
ρ = R × A / L. Silver has lowest resistivity (1.59×10⁻⁸) but is too expensive. Copper is the practical champion.
Heating elements use nichrome (ρ=1.1×10⁻⁶ Ω·m) — high resistivity converts electricity to heat. Copper (ρ=1.7×10⁻⁸) carries current without heating.
A copper wire 5 m long, A = 1×10⁻⁶ m², has R = 0.084 Ω. Find resistivity.
Correct! 1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m — copper's resistivity, standard for electrical wiring!
Adjust wire properties to see resistivity in action.
Deriving R_eq = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ for series
Sum of all resistances in series
Remember old Christmas lights where if ONE bulb burned out, the WHOLE string went dark? That is series wiring. V = V₁ + V₂ + V₃.
Using Ohm's law: I×Req = I×R₁ + I×R₂ + I×R₃. Same current flows through every bulb — only one path.
Req = R₁ + R₂ + R₃. Three 100Ω bulbs in series give 300Ω total. More bulbs = higher resistance = dimmer each becomes.
Old Christmas lights wired in series go dark when one bulb fails — the broken bulb breaks the only current path.
Three resistors 200Ω, 300Ω, 500Ω in series with 10V battery. Find total resistance.
Correct! 1000Ω. Current I = 10/1000 = 10 mA through all three.
Adjust resistors to see series equivalent.
Deriving c_l using electrical heating and calorimetry
Specific heat of liquid from electrical heating
You fill an electric kettle and switch it on. The heating element converts electrical energy to heat: Q_heater = V×I×t.
Heat warms both the water AND the metal calorimeter: Q_heater = m_l c_l ΔT + m_c c_c ΔT.
c_l = (VIt - m_c c_c ΔT) / (m_l ΔT). Subtract calorimeter share, divide by water mass and temperature rise.
An electric kettle heats 1L water from 20°C to 100°C needing 336,000 J. At 2000W, that takes about 168 seconds.
A 12V, 3A heater runs 150s in a calorimeter (m_c=0.08kg, c_c=900) containing 0.3kg liquid. Temp rises 22°C to 42°C. Find specific heat.
Correct! ~780 J/kg·K — typical for vegetable oils!
Use c_l = (VIt - m_c c_c ΔT) / (m_l ΔT)
Adjust values to see electrical calorimetry determine specific heat.
Deriving c_s using calorimetry and conservation of energy
Specific heat of solid from mixture method
You're making tea. Pour hot water into a cup and add cold milk. Heat lost = heat gained.
Q_s = m_s c_s (T₂ - T₃). The hot solid cools, losing heat.
Q_w + Q_c = (m_w c_w + m_c c_c)(T₃ - T₁). Both liquid and container absorb heat.
c_s = (m_w c_w + m_c c_c)(T₃ - T₁) / (m_s (T₂ - T₃)). All values measurable in lab.
Calorimetry in chemistry labs uses this principle — mixing known masses at known temperatures to find specific heat of unknown materials.
A 0.2kg metal at 90°C in 0.15kg water at 25°C. Calorimeter: 0.08kg, c_c=900. Final temp=28°C. Find c_s.
Use c_s = (m_w c_w + m_c c_c)(T₃ - T₁) / (m_s(T₂ - T₃))
Adjust values to see how calorimetry determines specific heat.
Deriving the temperature coefficient α of resistivity
Your phone dies faster on a freezing day. Resistance changes with temperature. Change in resistivity ∝ initial resistivity × ΔT.
ρ_T - ρ₀ = α ρ₀ ΔT. For copper, α is positive — resistance INCREASES when hot.
α = (ρ_T - ρ₀)/(ρ₀ ΔT). Copper α = 0.0039/°C. A copper wire heating 50°C increases resistance by 19.5%.
ρ_T = ρ₀[1 + α(T - T₀)]. Carbon and semiconductors have NEGATIVE α — used in thermistors for digital thermometers.
Incandescent bulbs have cold resistance ~40Ω but hot resistance ~400Ω — 10× increase explains the surge current when switched on.
Copper ρ₀=1.68×10⁻⁸ at 20°C. At 70°C, ρ_T=2.01×10⁻⁸. Find α.
Adjust values to find temperature coefficient.
Deriving I_p V_p = I_s V_s from conservation of power
Power plants generate at 25,000V. Transformers step up to 500,000V. Ideal transformer conserves power: Power_in = Power_out.
P = IV for both coils: I_p V_p = I_s V_s. High voltage = low current = low I²R heating losses.
I_s = (V_p/V_s) × I_p. Step-down transformer (500kV→230V) has V_p/V_s ≈ 2174. Current multiplies by 2174.
V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p = I_p/I_s. The voltage ratio equals turns ratio, inverse of current ratio.
Phone chargers use step-down transformers — 230V at wall becomes 5V at USB. Turns ratio 46:1 reduces voltage safely.
Step-down: V_p=240V, I_p=2A, V_s=24V. Find I_s.
Correct! 20A. Voltage steps down 10×, current steps up 10×. 480W = 480W.
Adjust voltages/current to see power conservation.
Deriving β = I_C/I_B and α = I_C/I_E
At a concert, the singer whispers into a mic and it fills the stadium. Inside, a tiny transistor amplifies. I_E = I_B + I_C.
β = I_C/I_B. Current gain typically 50-400. Tiny base current controls much larger collector current.
α = I_C/I_E, always slightly less than 1 (0.95-0.99). α = β/(β+1).
I_C = β × I_B. A 1μA base change produces 200μA collector change if β=200. Your smartphone has billions amplifying!
Hearing aids use transistors with β of 100+ — a tiny microphone signal (μA) drives a speaker at mA levels, amplifying 100×.
A transistor has I_B = 0.05 mA and I_C = 10 mA. Find β.
Correct! β = 200. 0.05 mA controls 10 mA — 200× amplification!
Adjust currents to see transistor gain.
Deriving β the coefficient of volumetric expansion
On a hot day, the fuel in your car's gas tank expands. ΔV ∝ V₀ × ΔT. Gas station tanks are calibrated for this.
ΔV = β V₀ ΔT. β is coefficient of volumetric expansion. Gasoline β ≈ 9.5×10⁻⁴/°C.
β = ΔV/(V₀ ΔT). For isotropic solids, β ≈ 3α. Volume = L³, so if length expands by (1+αΔT), volume expands by about (1+3αΔT).
β = 3α. This is why hot air balloons rise — air inside expands, becomes less dense, and floats.
Car engine thermostats use a wax pellet that expands with temperature — at 88°C it melts, opening the coolant valve to prevent overheating.
A 0.5 m³ alcohol tank at 10°C heats to 30°C. β=1.1×10⁻³/°C. Find ΔV.
Correct! 0.011 m³ (11 L). Liquids expand more than solids!
Adjust values to see volume expansion.

3. Rules for Converting to Indirect Speech
If the reporting verb (e.g., said, asked) is in the past tense, the tense in the reported clause generally shifts back one step in the past:
will ➔ would, can ➔ could, may ➔ might, must ➔ had to.
Pronouns change to reflect the perspective of the reporter rather than the original speaker.
First-person pronouns (I, we, my) change to match the subject of the reporting verb (he, she, they, his/her).
Second-person pronouns (you, your) change to match the object of the reporting verb (me, him, her, us).
Words indicating proximity in time and space shift to indicate distance:
Tomorrow ➔ The next day / The following day
Yesterday ➔ The previous day / The day before
When converting questions to indirect speech:
Change the reporting verb to "asked", "inquired", or "wondered".
Keep the original question word (who, what, where, when, why, how) as the connector.
Change the sentence structure from interrogative (verb-subject) back to declarative (subject-verb). Do not use a question mark.
Example (WH): He asked, "Where are you going?" ➔ He asked where I was going.
Example (Y/N): She asked, "Do you like tea?" ➔ She asked if I liked tea.

Morphology is the study of words, how they are formed, and their relationship to other words in the same language. It analyzes the structure of words and parts of words, such as stems, root words, prefixes, and suffixes.

A derivational suffix is added to a base word to create a new word with a different meaning or part of speech. For example, adding "-ness" to "happy" creates "happiness", changing an adjective to a noun.
When adding suffixes, spelling changes often occur in the base word:
If a word ends in a consonant + y, change the 'y' to 'i' before adding a suffix.
Drop the silent 'e' before adding a suffix that begins with a vowel.
If a word ends in a single consonant preceded by a single vowel, double the consonant before adding a suffix starting with a vowel.
